topic: A homeowner learns their house was a former crime scene
raw_output:
[story 1]

The smell hit me first—a faint, metallic tang beneath the usual scent of old wood and dust. I’d bought the house sight unseen, a bargain, a fixer-upper with character. Now, standing in the living room, tracing the outline of a faint, almost invisible scuff mark on the hardwood floor, the air seemed to thicken. My jaw clenched so tight I could feel the muscle jump beneath my skin. I slammed my hand onto the mantelpiece, the cheap ceramic catching with a sharp, jarring sound. "What the hell?" I muttered, the sound raw and uneven. Every shadow in the corners of the room suddenly looked like a lurking figure, and my pulse hammered against my ribs like a trapped bird. I started pacing, my boots scuffing the floorboards with aggressive, uneven strides, my eyes darting to every wall, searching for the evidence that wasn't there, yet somehow screamed at me.

[story 2]

I stared at the framed photograph on the dusty shelf. It was a picture of a smiling family, innocuous enough, until I noticed the slight discoloration near the edges, a pattern that looked suspiciously like dried blood. A cold, hard knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward into a burning pressure behind my eyes. I didn't scream; I couldn't. Instead, I grabbed the heavy iron poker from the fireplace, the cold metal shocking my palm. My breath hitched, coming in short, shallow gasps. "This isn't right," I whispered, the words barely audible over the sudden, deafening silence of the house. I spun around, kicking over a stack of old magazines. The resulting crash was loud, a deliberate act of disruption against the suffocating quiet, as I began methodically running my hands over the baseboards, searching for a seam, a crack, anything to prove this space had been something far uglier than a cozy home.

[story 3]

The realtor had assured me everything was clean, everything was standard. Lies. The realization hit me like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. I walked into the kitchen, the linoleum gleaming under the harsh overhead light, and focused on the pantry door. I didn't knock; I just shoved it open, the hinges protesting with a screech that seemed to echo the violence brewing inside me. Inside, behind the cans of forgotten spices, I found it: a faint, dark smear on the back wall, almost masked by decades of grime. My knuckles turned white as I gripped the edge of the cabinet. A tremor ran through my body, shaking my whole frame. I let out a guttural sound, a low growl that vibrated in my chest, and I slammed the cabinet shut with such force that the entire unit rattled. I backed away slowly, my eyes wide and fixed on the wall, unable to look away from the undeniable stain.
